sourceQuestion:
Let A be a two-dimensional array declared as follows:
A: array [1..10][1..15] of integer;
Assuming that each integer takes one memory location, the array is stored in row-major order and the first element of the array is stored at location 100, what is the address of the element A[i][j]?

A. 15i + j + 84
B. 15j + i + 84
C. 10i + j + 89
D. 10j + i + 89

parsed.stem: Let A be a two-dimensional array declared as follows:

options.text: array [1..10][1..15] of integer; Assuming that each integer takes one memory location, the array is stored in row-major order and the first element of the array is stored at location 100, what is the address of the element A[i][j]? A. 15i + j + 84

C. n^2 - 3n + 2
D. n^2(n-1)/2

sourceQuestion:
A program P reads in 500 integers in the range [0, 100] representing the scores of 500 students. It then prints the frequency of each score above 50. What would be the best way to store the frequencies?

A. An array of 50 numbers
B. An array of 100 numbers
C. An array of 101 numbers
D. A dynamically allocated array of 550 numbers

parsed.stem: A program P reads in 500 integers in the range [0, 100] representing the scores of 500 students. It then prints the frequency of each score above 50. What would be the best way to store the frequencies?

options.text: A dynamically allocated array of 550 numbers

It is an implementation of binary search.
It will always find the maximum element in listA.
It will return -1 even when x is present in listA.

sourceQuestion:
Let A be an array of n integers. Let t be the lowest upper bound on the number of comparisons of the array elements, required to find the minimum and maximum values in an arbitrary array of n elements. Which one of the following choices is correct?

A. t > 2n - 2
B. t > 3*floor(n/2) and t <= 2n - 2
C. t > floor(n/2) and t <= 3*floor(n/2)
D. t <= floor(n/2)

parsed.stem: Let A be an array of n integers. Let t be the lowest upper bound on the number of comparisons of the array elements, required to find the minimum and maximum values in an arbitrary array of n elements. Which one of the following choices is correct?

sourceQuestion:
Match the pairs in the following:

List 1:
(a) Heap construction with linear probing
(b) Constructing a Hashtable with linear probing
(c) AVL tree construction
(d) Digital trie construction

List 2:
(p) Theta(log_2(n))
(q) O(n)
(r) O(n^2)
(s) O(n log_2(n))

A. a-p, b-r, c-s, d-q
B. a-q, b-r, c-s, d-p
C. a-q, b-r, c-s, d-s
D. a-r, b-q, c-s, d-p

sourceQuestion:
In a binary tree, a full node is defined to be a node with 2 children. Use induction on the height of the binary tree to prove that the number of full nodes plus one is equal to the number of leaves.

Let F denote the number of full nodes and L denote the number of leaves. Which statement is to be proved?

A. F + 1 = L
B. F = L + 1
C. F = L
D. F = 2L

parsed.stem:

Let F denote the number of full nodes and L denote the number of leaves. Which statement is to be proved?

A. floor((i-1)/2)
B. floor(i/2)
C. floor((i+1)/2)
D. floor((i+2)/2)

A. Theta(n log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(1)

sourceQuestion:
The elements 32, 15, 20, 30, 12, 25, 16 are inserted one by one in the given order into a maxHeap. The resultant maxHeap is
A. (tree with 32 at root, 30 and 25 as children, 15 and 12 as left subtree leaves, 20 and 16 as right subtree leaves)
B. (tree with 32 at root, 30 and 25 as children, 15 and 12 and 20 and 16 at lower level)
C. (tree with 32 at root, 15 and 25 as children)
D. (tree with 32 at root, 30 and 25 as children, 15 and 12 at left, 20 and 16 at right)
sourceAnswer: A. 32 at root; left subtree: 30 (left child of root) with children 15 and 12; right subtree: 25 (right child of root) with children 20 and 16

parsed.stem: The elements 32, 15, 20, 30, 12, 25, 16 are inserted one by one in the given order into a maxHeap. The resultant maxHeap is

options.text: (tree with 32 at root, 30 and 25 as children, 15 and 12 as left subtree leaves, 20 and 16 as right subtree leaves)
(tree with 32 at root, 30 and 25 as children, 15 and 12 and 20 and 16 at lower level)
(tree with 32 at root, 15 and 25 as children)
(tree with 32 at root, 30 and 25 as children, 15 and 12 at left, 20 and 16 at right)

sourceQuestion:

A. 10, 7, 3, 2, 4, 1, 5
B. 10, 7, 3, 5, 4, 1, 2
C. 10, 5, 7, 2, 4, 1, 3
D. 10, 7, 5, 2, 4, 1, 3

A. O(n)
B. Theta(n log n)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(1)

sourceQuestion:
Statement for Linked Answer Questions 76 & 77:
A 3-ary max heap is like a binary max heap, but instead of 2 children, nodes have 3 children. A 3-ary heap can be represented by an array as follows: The root is stored in the first location a[1], nodes in the next level, from left to right, is stored from a[2] to a[4]. The nodes from the second level of the tree from left to right are stored from a[5] location onward. An item x can be inserted into a 3-ary heap containing n items by placing x in the location a[n+1] and pushing it up the tree to satisfy the heap property.

Q76. Which one of the following is a valid sequence of elements in an array representing a 3-ary max heap?

A. 1,3,6,5,9
B. 9,6,3,1,5
C. 9,3,6,1,5
D. 9,6,5,3,1

parsed.stem:

The nodes from the second level of the tree from left to right are stored from a[5] location onward. An item x can be inserted into a 3-ary heap containing n items by placing x in the location a[n+1] and pushing it up the tree to satisfy the heap property.

Q76. Which one of the following is a valid sequence of elements in an array representing a 3-ary max heap?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the process of inserting an element into a Max Heap, where the Max Heap is represented by an array. Suppose we perform a binary search on the path from the new leaf to the root to find the position for the newly inserted element, the number of comparisons performed is:

A. Theta(log_2(log_2(n)))
B. Theta(log_2(n))
C. Theta(sqrt(n))
D. Theta(n)

sourceQuestion:
A max-heap is a heap where the value of each parent is greater than or equal to the values of its children. Which of the following is a max-heap?

A. A tree with root 97, left child 17 (with left child 6 and right child 3), right child 46 (with left child 12 and right child 10)
B. A tree with root 97, left child 17 (with left child 6), right child 46 (with left child 12 and right child 10), and 6 has a right child 7
C. A tree with root 97, left child 46 (with left child 6 and right child 10), right child 17 (with left child 12 and right child 5)
D. A tree with root 97, left child 46, right child 17 (with left child 5), and 46 has left child 12 and right child 10 and 17 has right child 12
sourceAnswer: A. A tree with root 97, left child 17 (with left child 6 and right child 3), right child 46 (with left child 12 and right child 10)

parsed.stem: A max-heap is a heap where the value of each parent is greater than or equal to the values of its children. Which of the following is a max-heap?

options.text: A tree with root 97, left child 17 (with left child 6 and right child 3), right child 46 (with left child 12 and right child 10)
A tree with root 97, left child 17 (with left child 6), right child 46 (with left child 12 and right child 10), and 6 has a right child 7
A tree with root 97, left child 46 (with left child 6 and right child 10), right child 17 (with left child 12 and right child 5)
A tree with root 97, left child 46, right child 17 (with left child 5), and 46 has left child 12 and right child 10 and 17 has right child 12

sourceQuestion:
An operator delete(i) for a binary heap data structure is to be designed to delete the item in the i-th node. Assume that the heap is implemented in an array and i refers to the i-th index of the array. If the heap tree has depth d (number of edges on the path from the root to the farthest leaf), then what is the time complexity to re-fix the heap efficiently after the removal of the element?
A. O(d^2) but not O(d)
B. O(d) but not O(1)
C. O(2^d) but not O(d^2)
D. O(d * 2^d) but not O(2^d)

Assume that the heap is implemented in an array and i refers to the i-th index of the array. If the heap tree has depth d (number of edges on the path from the root to the farthest leaf), then what is the time complexity to re-fix the heap efficiently after the removal of the element?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements:
I. The smallest element in a max-heap is always at a leaf node.
II. The second largest element in a max-heap is always a child of a root node.
III. A max-heap can be constructed from a binary search tree in Theta(n) time.
IV. A binary search tree can be constructed from a max-heap in Theta(n log n) time.
Which of the above statements are TRUE?
A. I, II, and III
B. I, II, and IV
C. I, III, and IV
D. II, III, and IV

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements:
I. The smallest element in a max-heap is always at a leaf node.
II. The second largest element in a max-heap is always a child of a root node.
III. A max-heap can be constructed from a binary search tree in Theta(n) time.
IV. A binary search tree can be constructed from a max-heap in Theta(n log n) time.
Which of the above statements are TRUE?

D. Theta(n log n)

sourceQuestion:
An array of integers of size n can be converted into a heap by adjusting the heaps rooted at each internal node of the complete binary tree starting at the node floor(n/2), and doing this adjustment up to the root node (root node is at index 1) in the order floor(n/2), floor(n/2)-1, ..., 1. The time required to construct a heap in this manner is

A. O(log n)
B. O(n)
C. O(n log n)
D. O(n^2 log n)
